summary: debugcli typosquats `debug` and harvests installer credentials and browser wallets to attacker Telegram bot at install time
details: debugcli@4.3.5 impersonates the popular `debug` package (name/description mimic, and index.js is a passthrough `module.exports = require('debug')` so functional smoke tests pass). The declared `postinstall: node install.js` executes on `npm install` and performs multiple installer-harm actions: (1) reads a broad set of installer-owned credential files including ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.aws/config, ~/.npmrc, ~/.gitconfig, ~/.git-credentials, ~/.kube/config, ~/.docker/config.json, ~/.ssh/config, ~/.pypirc, and ~/.netrc, plus.env files walked up from cwd; (2) enumerates process.env and matches values against secret-keyword patterns (TOKEN, SECRET, AWS_, etc.); (3) locates Chrome Local Extension Settings for the MetaMask (nkbihfbeogaeaoehlefnkodbefgpgknn) and Trust Wallet (egjidjbpglichdcondbcbdnbeeppgdph) extensions and archives those vault directories via tar / PowerShell Compress-Archive; (4) collects host identifiers via os.hostname() and os.userInfo(); and (5) uploads all collected data to api.telegram.org using a hardcoded bot token and chat ID (bot 8938396680, chat 8055865368) via https.request. The install-time execution, breadth of installer-secret file reads, wallet-vault theft, and hardcoded attacker C2 endpoint together constitute an unambiguous supply-chain credential and cryptocurrency stealer.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
